retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius is a muscle comprised of particularly long muscle fibers spanning a large width of the upper back. Functionally, this allows the trapezius to assist in mainly postural attributes, allowing and supporting the spinal column to remain erect when the person is standing. The trapezius is one of the broadest and most superficial (closest to the skin) muscles of the upper back and trunk. It is often used as a landmark when dissecting a cadaver because it is encountered first. The trapezius is triangular, broad, and thin; it covers the upper back of the shoulders and neck. Its attachment points consist of the spinous process of C7-T12 of the spine, ligamentum nuchae, scapulae, clavicles, and ribs. While the trapezius muscle is mainly postural, it is also used for active movements such as side bending, rotation of the head, elevating and depressing the shoulders, and internally rotating the arm. The trapezius elevates, depresses, and retracts the scapula. The descending muscle fibers of the trapezius muscle internally rotate the arms. The transverse muscle fibers retract the scapulae, and the ascending muscle fibers medially rotate the scapulae.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The function of the trapezius is to stabilize and move the scapula. The upper fibers can elevate and upwardly rotate the scapula and extend the neck. The middle fibers adduct (medially retract) the scapula. The lower fibers depress and aid the upper fibers in upwardly rotating the scapula. These motions allow for the scapula to rotate against the levator scapulae and the rhomboid muscles. This rotation, in conjunction with the deltoid muscle, is essential for throwing objects.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius forms the posterior border of the posterior triangle of the neck. The sternocleidomastoid muscle (also innervated by the spinal accessory nerve) forms the anterior boundary. The clavicle completes the triangle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: One way to visualize the actions of the back and upper limb muscles is to visualize a large construction crane. The giant arm is the most obvious feature of the crane, but the jacks that anchor the cab of the crane to the ground are essential to its stability. Without these muscles, the upper limb (the arm of the crane) would be useless.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: To visualize the actions of the trapezius, consider a soldier coming to attention. The head is extended at the skull and neck (action of the upper portion of the trapezius). The scapulae are adducted, straightening the middle back and arms (action of the middle portion of the trapezius). The lower back and shoulder are extended (action of the inferior portion of the trapezius. The result is the "ramrod straight" posture. The unequal development of the three portions of the trapezius causes muscle imbalances and posture disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Extrinsic Muscles -- Structure and Function -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius works with other muscles to move the arm and stabilize the shoulder. The trapezius assists the levator scapulae in lifting the scapular bone. The serratus anterior and trapezius work together to rotate the scapula superiorly, allowing the arm to reach above the head. Joint actions by the trapezius and rhomboids retract the scapula to the posterior midline, stabilizing the shoulders during motion. The trapezius helps cervical muscles move the head and neck posteriorly and laterally. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The thenar muscles are a group of three muscles that act on the thumb. These muscles form the bulge on the palmar surface of the thumb and palm, called the thenar eminence. The largest of the three muscles, opponens pollicis , originates at the tubercle of the trapezium and inserts at the lateral margin of the metacarpal of the thumb. It permits the thumb to perform opposition which is the motion of reaching across the palm towards the little finger, by flexing and medially rotating the metacarpal on the axis of the trapezium. The abductor pollicis brevis muscle is positioned anterior to the opponens pollicis and is the primary muscle providing the act of opposition. It originates at the tubercles of the scaphoid and trapezium and inserts at the lateral aspect of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. Abductor pollicis brevis also acts by drawing the thumb away from the midline, which is the action of abduction for all muscles. The f lexor pollicis brevis originates at the tubercle of the trapezium via the deep head, and the associated flexor retinaculum via the superficial head, and inserts at the base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb. These three muscles are all innervated by the recurrent branch of the median nerve. The flexor pollicis brevis receives dual innervation with fibers from both the median and ulnar nerves. The superficial head is innervated by the median nerve while the deep head is innervated by the ulnar nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The latissimus dorsi is a powerful extensor of the arm at the shoulder. The rhomboid major and minor serve to adduct the scapula. They also adduct and extend the arm at the shoulder, as well as depressing the scapula, and rotating the arm medially.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The two muscles in the superficial layer include the splenius cervicis and splenius capitis. They help with movements of the shoulder and neck. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Shoulder Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius is a large triangular-shaped muscle that overlies the shoulder posteriorly. The trapezius originates from the superior aspect of the nuchal line in the occipital, cervical, and upper thoracic region and inserts at the lateral aspect of the clavicle, the acromion, and spine of the scapula. The function of the trapezius muscle is both elevation and depression of the shoulder depending on whether the upper or lower muscle fibers are activated.  When the entire trapezius muscle contracts, the fibers are geometrically opposed, and the forces are balanced, resulting in no shoulder movement.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Trapezius -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Thus, there are three components of the trapezius muscle. [1] The superior portion attaches to the skull (external occipital protuberance and superior nuchal line) and neck (ligamentum nuchae). This portion of the muscle extends the head at the neck. The middle portion attaches to the upper part of the spinous processes of C7-T12. This portion adducts (draws together) the two scapulae. The inferior portion (lower part of C7-T12) depresses the scapula. Muscular insertions are onto the scapular spine, acromion process, and the lateral third of the clavicle. The axio-appendicular muscles attach the body wall to the arm. The real significance of the functions of the muscles that attach the body wall to the scapula is that they fix the scapula so that the scapulohumeral muscles that attach the scapula to the arm can function effectively. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Shoulder Evaluation Strategies -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Trapezius
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius is a large triangular-shaped muscle that overlies the shoulder posteriorly. The trapezius originates from the superior aspect of the nuchal line in the occipital, cervical, and upper thoracic region and inserts at the lateral aspect of the clavicle, the acromion, and spine of the scapula. The function of the trapezius muscle is both elevation and depression of the shoulder, depending on whether the upper or lower muscle fibers are activated.  When the entire trapezius muscle contracts, the fibers are geometrically opposed, and the forces are balanced, resulting in no movement of the shoulder.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Rhomboid Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The rhomboids consist of two separate muscles; the major and minor muscles, which are found immediately deep to the trapezius. The rhomboid minor is a cylindrical muscle that originates at the ligamentum nuchae and C7 and T1 vertebra. It inserts at the scapula's medial border near the base of the spine of the scapula. The rhomboid major is a quadrangular muscle located inferior to the rhomboid minor. The origin of the rhomboid muscles is from the spinous processes of the T2-T5 vertebra and inserts on the medial border of the scapula, just inferior to the rhomboid minor. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Back, Muscles -- Muscles -- Splenius Cervicis
retrieved_docs.content: Origin: T3-T6 spinous processes Insertion: transverse processes of atlas and axis, posterior tubercle of the C3 vertebra Action: Unilateral: rotation of the upper cervical vertebra Bilateral: extension of the upper cervical spine Relationships: Deep to serratus posterior superior, rhomboids and trapezius Superficial to parts of erector spinae and semispinalis muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Levator Scapulae Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The levator scapulae muscle originates from the posterior tubercles of transverse processes of C1 (atlas), C2 (axis), C3, and C4 vertebrae. [1] [2] The muscle inserts on the posterior lip of the medial scapular border, typically between the superior angle and root of the scapular spine. [3] [4] The sternocleidomastoid and trapezius overlay the superior and inferior aspects of the levator scapulae, respectively, with the levator scapulae comprising part of the floor of the posterior triangle of the neck. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Arm Abductor Muscles -- Muscles -- Trapezius and Serratus Anterior
retrieved_docs.content: The trapezius and serratus anterior muscles work in tandem to coordinate rotation and movement of the scapula to accommodate the full range of motion of the arm. Specifically, they facilitate abduction of the arm from 90 degrees and further upwards.

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate a structured framework that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic, surgical, or injection‑based treatments for tendon and tendon‑sheath conditions of the hand and other musculoskeletal sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Hand and wrist incisional surgeries are organized under the “Hand, Wrist: Incision” category, which is nested within both “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Specific procedures in this group include aspiration of the bursa of hand, aspiration of fascia of hand, division of the joint capsule of hand, division of the joint capsule of wrist, drainage of tendon of hand, puncture of the bursa of hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, release for tenosynovitis of the extensor (external) pollicis brevis, and release of the tendon sheath of the hand. Aspiration of the bursa of hand is also classified under “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue,” reflecting its minimally invasive fluid‑removal role, while aspiration of fascia of hand is listed as a “Procedure on musculoskeletal system,” emphasizing its musculoskeletal operative relevance. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 57
retrieved_docs.content: hand surgery apply Excision to tendon‑sheath pathologies, including excision of ganglion, giant‑cell tumor, lesion, and xanthoma of the hand tendon sheath, as well as synovectomy and tenectomy of the hand tendon sheath, highlighting the structure “tendon sheath within hand” as a focal target for multiple hand‑specific interventions. Additional exploration‑related Excision examples include laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, radical orchiectomy for testicular tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroid surgery ranging from simple parathyroidectomy to combined mediastinal, sternal‑split, or transthoracic exploration of ectopic or mediastinal parathyroid tissue. Excision also relates to “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and to plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its relevance to head‑and‑neck and cosmetic/reconstructive surgery. Collectively, these procedures demonstrate that

retrieved_docs.content: falls under both “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “Operative procedures on the upper extremity: wrist and hand.” Its child actions include puncture of the bursa of the hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, and release for tenosynovitis of the extensor pollicis brevis. Related incision‑based interventions—aspiration of the bursa of the hand, aspiration of the fascia of the hand (an inverse‑isa of Procedure on musculoskeletal system), division of the joint capsule of the hand or wrist, and drainage of a tendon of the hand—are grouped within “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue.” Division of the joint capsule is an isa of both hand and wrist capsule divisions and is linked to the broader “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” which parents “Incision and excision of joint structures” and the overall “Operation on musculoskeletal system.” Incision and excision of joint structures

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 217

In a parallel branch, “Operation on bursa” functions as a general category that includes the more specific “Puncture of bursa of hand,” illustrating an inverse‑is‑a relationship where the puncture is a particular form of bursal operation. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy of diagnostic and therapeutic procedures on muscles, tendons, tendon sheaths and bursae, connecting individual hand‑focused surgeries to their respective broader procedural families and supporting organized clinical documentation and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy, which may be performed as either a partial (lobectomy) or a complete (total) removal of thyroid tissue.

The procedure “Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of hand” is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” group. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured framework that links specific aspiration, diagnostic, exploratory, irrigation, release, and removal actions to their respective procedural categories, anatomical sites, and linguistic equivalents, facilitating coherent understanding and management of musculoskeletal procedures in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of the hand is regarded as a therapeutic intervention that is closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. The broader category “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” functions as a classification that encompasses such specific interventions, including the removal of rice bodies from hand tendon sheaths. This relationship indicates that the hand‑specific procedure is considered part of the general group of muscle and tendon therapies, reflecting its inclusion within the overall taxonomy of musculoskeletal therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1914
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of fascia of hand is a medical procedure performed on the musculoskeletal system, specifically targeting the fascial tissue of the hand for removal of fluid or other material. This intervention is classified as a procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reflecting its role in diagnosing or treating conditions affecting hand fascia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1913
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of bursa of hand is a medical procedure that is classified within the broader category of injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints, and soft tissue.

Together, these relationships illustrate how the clavicle’s extensive surgical options and the aorta’s vascular procedures are contextualized within detailed anatomical, developmental, and pharmacologic frameworks, and how the “SHOULDER AND ARM: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category unifies a broad spectrum of shoulder‑ and arm‑related operative techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 629
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of the bursa of the hand and aspiration of the fascia of the hand are both minimally invasive procedures that fall under the broader category of injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints, and soft‑tissue structures. These aspiration techniques are classified as hand‑wrist incision procedures, linking them to the operative domain of the upper extremity. The “Hand, Wrist: Incision” group is itself a subcategory of the comprehensive set of operative procedures on the wrist and hand, which is organized under the overarching classification of operative procedures on the upper extremity. Within this hierarchy, the hand‑wrist incision category includes specific release operations for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus and of the extensor pollicis brevis, indicating that these releases are particular types of upper‑extremity operative procedures. Additionally, aspiration of the fascia of the hand is recognized as a type of procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reinforcing its placement within the musculoskeletal procedural taxonomy. Together, these relationships illustrate how aspiration and release techniques for hand and wrist conditions are integrated into a structured framework of upper‑extremity surgical and minimally invasive interventions, facilitating clear categorization for clinical documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.
